retrieved_docs.title: Pennsylvania Child Abuse Recognition and Reporting -- Pearls and Other Issues -- A 16-year-old child presents to the emergency department due to acting out at school. Shortly after arrival, the child asks for a sandwich. The school could not reach the biological mother, and the biological father is deceased. The child indicates his mother is on a honeymoon on a boat somewhere in the Cayman Islands. He is currently being taken care of by his grandmother, who is at work as a dietician, but she will be home shortly. The mother left him with $200 10 days ago and told him to buy local fast food for both of them for dinner. He admits he was told not to spend more than $20 daily and avoid dessert due to his grandmother's diabetes. He says he is out of money because he bought dessert daily and spent $30 instead of $20. He says he is "starving." There is no food in the house besides the fruits, vegetables, and frozen meals his mother provided. Which of the following would be an appropriate response of the emergency department health providers?
retrieved_docs.content: The child should be given food, and the grandmother should be contacted. Additionally, while there may not be reasonable cause to suspect child abuse, the PA CPSL prescribes that a person, hospital, institution, school, facility, agency, or agency employee acting in good faith shall have immunity from civil and criminal liability that might otherwise result from making a report of suspected child abuse or making a referral for general protective services, regardless of whether the report is required to be made under the PA CPSL. General concerns for a child's safety, health, and well-being may be reported to ChildLine for assessment of services and support.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Effects on Child Health(Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Environmental Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Changes in the environment in the past few decades in terms of easy access/ affordability of high-calorie fast food, increased portion size, increased intake of sugary beverages, and sedentary lifestyles are associated with increased incidence of obesity. [10] Increasing use of electronic devices [television, tablets, smartphone, videogames] by children has led to limited physical activity, disruption of the sleep-wake cycle, depression of metabolic rate, and poor eating patterns. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Anti-Inflammatory Diets -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Low-income households repeatedly attributed economic burden as a barrier to initiating a healthier diet. [24] [25] [26] Low-income households purchase nutrient-dense foods, such as fruits, vegetables, and lean meats, less frequently due to the perceived high cost. [27] Implementing a healthy diet into a household, particularly one that includes children, presents added challenges. Calorie-dense foods such as processed meats, cereals, and pre-packaged, boxed, or ready-to-eat meals contain more fats and sugars but are usually less expensive than nutrient-dense foods. [24] Calorie-dense foods are frequently easier to prepare, satisfy hunger more quickly, and may be more palatable to young children, adding to the purchase appeal. [24] Households where adolescents and adults work long hours, often at multiple jobs, have limited time to purchase food and prepare meals. [24] Residents of many lower-income communities are affected by food insecurity and frequently live in food deserts characterized by limited access to grocery stores or fresh food; the time required to access healthy foods, often using unreliable or limited transportation methods, adds to overall food costs. [25] [26] These factors pose significant challenges for patients to initiate a healthier diet and must be accounted for by healthcare providers when recommending this treatment and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity in Pediatric Patients -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Weight gain occurs when energy intake chronically exceeds energy expenditure, with both measured in calories, eventually leading to obesity. [5] Genetic determinants play a lesser role. Shifting food preferences in recent decades have been influenced by marketing and the increased availability of high-calorie, processed foods, such as fast food and sugar-sweetened beverages. The widespread consumption of hyperpalatable, energy-dense foods, combined with larger portion sizes and frequent snacking, has been strongly associated with the rising prevalence of obesity in industrialized nations. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Evaluation -- Identification of Patients at Risk of Fatal or Near-Fatal Asthma
retrieved_docs.content: Minor risk factors include exposure to aeroallergens and tobacco smoke, illicit drug use, older patients, aspirin sensitivity, long duration of asthma, and frequent hospitalizations for asthma-related issues.

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: White non-Hispanic adults are more likely to have allergic rhinitis, whereas Black adults and children have higher rates of food allergies. Asthma is most common among American Indian, Alaska Native, and Black populations. Globally, the lifetime prevalence of anaphylaxis ranges from 0.05% to 2% in adults and 0.04% to 1.8% in children. This condition accounts for up to 0.26% of hospital admissions, with a mortality rate of 0.5% to 1%. [17]  Anaphylaxis triggers vary by age—food in young children, insect venom in school-aged children, and medications in teenagers. [17] Peanuts and tree nuts are the leading causes of fatal anaphylaxis in children, whereas cow's milk and seafood are also significant triggers in specific populations.

retrieved_docs.title: History Taking in Obesity Medicine: A Comprehensive Guide -- Function -- Special Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Other nutritional factors that contribute to obesity that should be assessed include: Food insecurity screening Limited access to nutritious food Compensatory behaviors (binge eating, high-calorie shelf-stable items) Impact on the feasibility of dietary recommendations Ultra-processed food assessment Consumption of packaged snacks, sugary beverages, and fast food Restaurant meal frequency Vending machine use patterns

retrieved_docs.title: Status Asthmaticus -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Two distinct patterns of asthma exacerbations can lead to status asthmaticus. Slow-onset asthma exacerbation involves a gradual worsening of peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) over several days. Predisposing patient-related factors include inadequate inhaler regimens, suboptimal compliance, and psychological stressors. Sudden-onset asthma exacerbation presents as a rapid, severe decline within hours, often triggered by abrupt massive exposure to allergens, food particles, or sulfites. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Food Allergies -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Reports reveal significant disparities in food allergy outcomes, with racial and ethnic minorities and individuals living in high-poverty areas experiencing higher rates of emergency department visits, hospitalizations, and fatalities due to food-induced anaphylaxis. Multiple studies have documented increased diagnostic coding for food-induced anaphylaxis among Black, Hispanic, and Asian children compared to White children. Black individuals, in particular, are disproportionately affected by comorbid asthma—a known risk factor for fatal anaphylaxis. Mortality data further reveal a greater burden of fatal reactions among Black individuals, especially males. [37] These findings underscore the urgent need for targeted public health strategies to address inequities in food allergy diagnosis, management, and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Environmental Factors and Obesity -- Function -- Social and Economic Factors
retrieved_docs.content: The effect of globalization and economic changes over the past decade has generated a decreased cost and growing prevalence of fast food as well as a decrease in physical activity. On the other hand, food accessibility is also a major factor. Especially in the United States, access to affordable healthy food varies by ethnic, racial, and socioeconomic status. In minority predominant and lower-income neighborhoods, there are approximately 30% fewer supermarkets. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The worldwide incidence of asthma is estimated to affect 260 million individuals. [20] Recent studies examining asthma prevalence across 17 countries reveal varying rates, ranging from 3.4% to 6% for adults and children in India, Taiwan, Kosovo, Nigeria, and Russia, and higher rates of 17% to 33% for Honduras, Costa Rica, Brazil, and New Zealand. [21] Despite data showing the death rate consistently declining for asthma between 2001 and 2015, asthma continues to account for approximately 420,000 deaths per year. [22] Factors such as under-prescription of inhaled glucocorticoids and limited access to emergency medical care or specialist care all play a role in asthma-related deaths.

retrieved_docs.title: History Taking in Obesity Medicine: A Comprehensive Guide -- Issues of Concern -- Environmental and geographic assessment
retrieved_docs.content: The built environment has a significant impact on obesity risk through its influence on food access, physical activity opportunities, and exposure to environmental stressors. [35] Food deserts, defined as areas with limited access to affordable and nutritious food, affect approximately 23.5 million people in the United States. [USDA Nutritious Food Access] Environmental health assessment evaluates the following components: Food environment Distance to full-service grocery stores Density of fast-food restaurants Availability of farmers' markets or community gardens School and workplace food environments Physical activity environment Safe walking/cycling infrastructure Access to parks, recreation centers, and gyms Neighborhood safety and lighting Public transportation availability Housing and safety Housing stability and overcrowding Neighborhood violence and safety concerns Environmental toxin exposure Air quality and pollution levels

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Evaluation -- Identification of Patients at Risk of Fatal or Near-Fatal Asthma
retrieved_docs.content: Most asthma-related deaths are preventable if risk factors are identified and addressed early. Major risk factors that place patients at high risk for future fatal asthma exacerbations include: A recent history of poorly controlled asthma A prior history of near-fatal asthma A history of endotracheal intubation for asthma A history of intensive care unit admission for asthma

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medications -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Asthma is a very common illness in the developed world. It impacts approximately 23 million people in the United States with as many as 7 million of these being adolescents. Worldwide, over 300 million are impacted daily with as many as 250,000 deaths associated with asthma. In the United States, morbidity is greater in the Black as opposed to the White population.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity as a Chronic Disease: Epidemiology and Pathophysiologic Insights -- Epidemiology -- Geographic Pattern Analysis
retrieved_docs.content: The 18.7 percentage point difference between the highest (West Virginia) and lowest (District of Columbia) obesity rates reflects complex interactions of the following factors: Socioeconomic factors : Income inequality, educational attainment, employment opportunities Food environment : Access to healthy foods, density of fast-food establishments, food pricing Built environment : Walkability, recreational facilities, urban planning, transportation options Cultural factors : Regional food traditions, attitudes toward physical activity, social norms Healthcare access : Clinician availability, insurance coverage, specialized obesity services

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Acute Exacerbation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients experiencing an acute asthma exacerbation may manage symptoms at home or require urgent medical care, depending on severity and risk factors for fatal asthma. Urgent medical attention is needed for those with marked shortness of breath, inability to speak more than short phrases, use of accessory muscles, mental status changes, or at high risk of fatal exacerbation, which requires urgent medical attention. Risk factors that predict a fatal asthma exacerbation include: Previous life-threatening exacerbation Exacerbation despite OCS use Multiple emergency room visits (3 or more) or hospitalizations (more than 1) in the last year Use of more than 1 SABA MDI per month Food allergies Chronic heart or lung disease Medication nonadherence Psychosocial stresssors

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2644
retrieved_docs.content: Allergic reactions represent a broad category of immune‑mediated responses, and chronic allergic rhinitis (الْتِهابُ الأَنْفِ الأَرَجِيّ الدائم) is a specific form of such reactions. Foods (potraviny) are common triggers that can precipitate allergic reactions, and bran, being a type of food, may act as an allergen in susceptible individuals. Recognizing that certain foods can provoke allergic responses, including chronic allergic rhinitis, is important for patient counseling, avoidance strategies, and overall management of allergic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2851
retrieved_docs.content: Asthma and the Arabic‑named condition الرَبْو are clinically similar, indicating they represent the same respiratory disorder. Exposure to air pollutants, described as znečištění ovzduší, is related to the development or exacerbation of asthma (الرَبْو). Tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ) is also clinically associated with asthma, contributing to symptom worsening and disease progression. Additionally, the sodium salt of ibuprofen (sodná sůl ibuprofenu) is contraindicated in patients with asthma, as it may provoke bronchospasm or other adverse respiratory effects. Recognizing these environmental and pharmacologic factors is important for managing asthma effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1442
retrieved_docs.content: Asthma and the Arabic term الرَبْو refer to the same respiratory disease and are clinically similar. Clinical observations indicate that elevated blood‑glucose levels (ارتفاع السكر في الدم) are associated with asthma, suggesting that patients with hyperglycemia may have an increased risk of asthma‑related complications. Environmental factors also play a role, as air‑pollution exposure (znečištění ovzduší) is related to the development and exacerbation of asthma. Regarding pharmacotherapy, sodium ibuprofen (sodná sůl ibuprofenu) is contraindicated in asthma because non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drugs can trigger bronchospasm in susceptible individuals. In contrast, cromolyn sodium (kromoglykát dvojsodný) is not contraindicated; it is a mast‑cell stabilizer that is commonly used to prevent asthma attacks, and the suggestion of a contraindication appears to be a misinterpretation. Understanding these interconnections—between metabolic status, environmental exposure, and medication safety—is essential for comprehensive asthma management and patient care.

Cromolyn is also employed in the prophylaxis of الربو الناشئ عن التمارين الرياضية (exercise‑induced asthma), where its preventive effect helps to avert bronchoconstriction triggered by physical activity. Although cromolyn is generally safe for asthma management, it may be contraindicated in certain severe or uncontrolled حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة (asthmatic conditions), reflecting the need for careful patient selection when using this agent. The combination product isoproterenol + cromolyn further illustrates cromolyn’s role as an active ingredient alongside a β‑adrenergic agonist, providing both bronchodilatory (via isoproterenol) and anti‑inflammatory (via cromolyn) actions. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 24
retrieved_docs.content: Cromolyn is an active ingredient present in several combination products, including those that pair it with albuterol or isoproterenol, and these formulations are classified under the Miscellaneous Drugs category, indicating that cromolyn is a child of this broader drug class. The specific formulation cromolyn sodium 40 mg/mL ophthalmic solution represents the active moiety of cromolyn. Pharmacologically, cromolyn produces physiologic effects by decreasing histamine activity and decreasing leukotriene activity, mechanisms that are relevant to conditions such as mast‑cell hyperplasia. Clinically, cromolyn is used to prevent exercise‑induced asthma and to treat food‑related allergic reactions, while it is contraindicated in patients with an underlying asthmatic (bronchial) condition. Combination products that contain both isoproterenol and cromolyn list these two agents as the active ingredients. Within the same Miscellaneous Drugs group, chlorbutanol is also a child drug, and metyrosine is listed as another child of this category. Hydrocortisone sodium succinate, a corticosteroid preparation, is employed in the management of severe asthma (bronchial) episodes, circulatory collapse, and other acute inflammatory states. Quercetin, a flavonoid, may be used to treat food allergy, aligning with cromolyn’s role in managing similar allergic conditions. Together, cromolyn, hydrocortisone sodium succinate, chlorbutanol, metyrosine, and quercetin illustrate a network of agents within the miscellaneous drug class, each linked by therapeutic indications related to allergic, inflammatory, and emergency cardiovascular or respiratory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 597
retrieved_docs.content: Cromolyn is a mast‑cell‑stabilizing agent that appears as an active ingredient in several combination pharmaceutical products, including “Albuterol and cromolyn only product,” “Albuterol‑ and cromolyn‑containing product,” “Cromolyn and isoproterenol only product,” and “Cromolyn‑ and isoproterenol‑containing product.” A formulation containing both isoproterenol and cromolyn also lists cromolyn as an active component, and the ophthalmic preparation “cromolyn sodium 40 MG/ML Ophthalmic Solution” contains the active moiety cromolyn. Pharmacologically, cromolyn produces a physiologic effect of decreased leukotriene activity, which contributes to its ability to reduce mast‑cell hyperplasia (“كثرة الخلايا البدينة”). Clinically, cromolyn may be used to treat food allergy (“الحساسية الغذائية”), although its effectiveness can be limited in patients with exercise‑induced asthma (“الربو الناشئ عن التمارين الرياضية”). In addition, the flavonoid quercetin is noted to have the potential to treat food allergy, offering an alternative or adjunctive therapeutic option. These interconnections highlight cromolyn’s role across multiple drug formulations, its mechanism of action via leukotriene inhibition and mast‑cell stabilization, and its relevance to managing allergic conditions while considering specific contraindications such as exercise‑induced asthma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1783
retrieved_docs.content: Asthma (الربو) is recognized as clinically similar to the condition described by the Arabic term الرَبْو, underscoring that both refer to the same respiratory disorder. The disease is closely linked to environmental and lifestyle factors: exposure to air pollution (znečištění ovzduší) and the habit of smoking (تعاطي التَبغ) are both clinically associated with worsening or triggering asthma symptoms. In addition, asthma frequently co‑occurs with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (مرض الانسداد الرئوي المزمن), indicating a significant overlap in their clinical presentations and shared risk factors. Certain medications are contraindicated in patients with asthma; specifically, ibuprofen lysine, sodium ibuprofen salt (sodná sůl ibuprofenu), and the double‑sodium salt of cromolyn (kromoglykát dvojsodný) should be avoided because they may exacerbate bronchospasm or other asthma‑related complications. Understanding these interrelationships helps clinicians anticipate triggers, avoid harmful drugs, and manage asthma more effectively in the context of related respiratory conditions and environmental exposures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Asthma is clinically similar to the condition described by the Arabic term الرَبْو, indicating that the two share comparable clinical features. The condition الرَبْو is also clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that its presence may increase the risk of adverse outcomes in newborns. Environmental factors such as znečištění ovzduší (air pollution) are related to الرَبْو, highlighting the role of polluted air in the development or exacerbation of this condition. Additionally, the medication kromoglykát dvojsodný (dipotassium cromoglycate) is contraindicated in patients with الرَبْو, indicating that its use should be avoided in the presence of this disease to prevent potential harm.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2923
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑asthmatic therapy involves the corticosteroid beclomethasone, which is classified as an inhaled steroid agent (ISA) and is used to manage airway disease. Beclomethasone specifically targets inflammation (الالتهاب), reducing inflammatory processes in the respiratory tract and thereby improving asthma control. This highlights beclomethasone’s central role as an anti‑asthmatic medication that treats inflammatory airway conditions.
